heroOverview: A hiccough — commonly spelled hiccup — is an involuntary, repetitive contraction of the diaphragm followed by sudden closure of the vocal cords, producing the characteristic sound. Each episode lasts a fraction of a second, but persistent hiccoughs lasting more than 48 hours affect roughly 1 in 100,000 people and can signal serious underlying pathology such as central nervous system disease, metabolic disturbance, or structural irritation of the vagus or phrenic nerves. In healthy adults, hiccoughs resolve spontaneously within minutes to hours. When they continue beyond two days, formal medical evaluation is warranted. Hiccoughs lasting more than one month are classified as intractable and demand thorough investigation. Although a nuisance in their common benign form, persistent cases can impair sleep, nutrition, and quality of life.

keyStats.prevalence: Benign self-limited hiccoughs affect virtually everyone; persistent cases (>48 hours) are rare, estimated at fewer than 1 per 100,000 person-years
keyStats.demographics: Intractable hiccoughs are more common in men and in people with underlying neurological or metabolic disease
keyStats.avgAge: No specific peak; gastroesophageal reflux-driven episodes are common across adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global incidence unknown; most cases are transient and never reach clinical attention

definition: Hiccough (ICD-10: R06.6) is defined as a sudden, involuntary clonic contraction of the diaphragm and external intercostal muscles occurring synchronously with abrupt closure of the glottis, which arrests inspiration and generates the recognisable sound. The neural arc involves the vagus nerve (cranial nerve X), the phrenic nerve (C3-C5), and a putative hiccough reflex centre thought to reside in the upper spinal cord between C3 and C5, with additional modulation from the brainstem and higher cortical centres. Hiccoughs are classified by duration: benign or transient (lasting minutes to 48 hours), persistent (48 hours to one month), and intractable (beyond one month). The benign form is triggered by gastric distension, sudden temperature changes, or emotional excitement and resolves without treatment. Persistent and intractable hiccoughs require a structured search for an underlying cause, which can involve virtually any system — gastrointestinal, pulmonary, cardiovascular, metabolic, neurological, or pharmacological. Hiccoughs fall under general medicine as a presenting symptom; specific cause-directed management may involve gastroenterology, neurology, or respiratory medicine depending on the underlying aetiology identified.

typesClassification.description: Last fewer than 48 hours, arise from gastric distension, sudden alcohol or food ingestion, rapid eating, or emotional stimulation, and resolve spontaneously without medical intervention.

Continue for 48 hours to one month; typically reflect a specific organic cause — most commonly gastroesophageal reflux disease, esophageal or gastric disease, or a metabolic disturbance — and require investigation.

Last longer than one month; suggest a serious underlying condition such as a central nervous system lesion, severe metabolic derangement, diaphragmatic irritation, or a side effect of medications such as corticosteroids or chemotherapy.
Very common in newborns, especially after feeding; considered physiologically normal and generally resolve without treatment as the central nervous system matures.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, repetitive, involuntary diaphragmatic contractions producing the characteristic sound, typically occurring at a rate of 4-60 per minute.
Abrupt interruption of breathing during each episode as the glottis closes, causing a brief pause in respiration.
Chest tightness or discomfort during prolonged bouts as the diaphragm and intercostal muscles fatigue.
Throat irritation or a tickling sensation in the back of the throat accompanying each contraction.
Disrupted speech — the hiccough interrupts phonation, causing difficulty finishing sentences.
Difficulty sleeping when hiccoughs persist through the night, leading to fragmented rest and daytime fatigue.
Nausea or a feeling of fullness when hiccoughs are associated with gastric distension or reflux.
Heartburn or regurgitation when hiccoughs co-occur with gastroesophageal reflux disease.
Reduced appetite and difficulty eating in prolonged cases, since chewing and swallowing exacerbate episodes.
Psychological distress — embarrassment, anxiety, and social withdrawal in individuals with persistent or intractable episodes.
earlyWarningSigns: Hiccoughs persisting beyond 48 hours without an identifiable benign trigger such as rapid eating or cold drinks.
Recurrent hiccough bouts waking the patient from sleep, suggesting organic rather than functional origin.
Hiccoughs accompanied by new neurological symptoms such as headache, difficulty swallowing, or limb weakness.
Hiccoughs in the context of recent surgery, anaesthesia, or chemotherapy initiation.
emergencySigns: Sudden-onset persistent hiccoughs in a patient with risk factors for stroke, brain tumour, or recent head injury.
Hiccoughs with concurrent vomiting, altered consciousness, or signs of raised intracranial pressure.
Hiccoughs combined with chest pain, shortness of breath, or diaphoresis suggesting a cardiac event.
Intractable hiccoughs causing inability to maintain adequate oral intake, leading to dehydration or significant weight loss.
Hiccoughs following thoracic, abdominal, or spinal surgery not responding to first-line management.

causes.description: Rapid eating, swallowing air (aerophagia), carbonated beverages, or overfilling the stomach stretches the gastric fundus, which directly abuts the diaphragm and reflexively triggers phrenic nerve activation, producing the hiccough arc. This is by far the most common precipitant of benign transient episodes.

Acid reflux irritates the lower oesophageal mucosa and stimulates vagal afferents, activating the hiccough reflex arc. GERD is one of the leading identifiable causes of persistent hiccoughs in outpatient practice; treatment of the reflux commonly resolves the hiccoughs.

Structural lesions abutting the phrenic nerve in the neck, mediastinum, or diaphragm — including mediastinal lymphadenopathy, aortic aneurysm, lung tumours, pericarditis, and subphrenic abscess — or vagal irritation anywhere along its thoracoabdominal course can perpetuate the reflex arc. These account for many intractable cases.

Brainstem infarcts, tumours, multiple sclerosis plaques, or encephalitis affecting the area postrema or the putative hiccough centre in the upper cervical cord can produce persistent or intractable hiccoughs by disrupting normal reflex inhibition. Medullary strokes are a classic cause.

Uraemia (elevated blood urea nitrogen in chronic kidney disease), hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, and hypercalcaemia lower the threshold for the hiccough reflex by altering neuronal membrane excitability. These metabolic triggers should be measured in every patient with unexplained persistent hiccoughs.

Corticosteroids (especially after intravenous pulse dosing), opioids, benzodiazepines, and several chemotherapy agents — including cisplatin and carboplatin — are well-recognised pharmacological triggers. Medication review is a key early step in evaluation of persistent hiccoughs.

Subphrenic abscess, peritonitis, hepatomegaly, splenic enlargement, or post-surgical irritation of the diaphragmatic peritoneum activates the phrenic afferents locally and can sustain persistent hiccoughs that resolve only when the underlying abdominal process is treated.

riskFactors.description: Persistent and intractable hiccoughs are reported approximately four times more often in men than women across published case series; the biological basis is incompletely understood but may relate to differences in vagal tone and oesophageal physiology.
Active GERD is one of the most frequently identified modifiable causes of persistent hiccoughs; appropriate acid-suppression therapy both treats and prevents recurrent episodes.

Alcohol causes rapid gastric distension and oesophageal mucosal irritation, acutely triggering hiccoughs, while chronic alcohol use can cause oesophagitis and central nervous system effects that perpetuate them.

Brainstem or cervical cord pathology, including multiple sclerosis, stroke, and tumour, removes normal inhibitory modulation of the hiccough reflex and is responsible for a substantial proportion of intractable cases.
Metabolic waste accumulation in advanced renal failure lowers the seizure and reflex threshold; dialysis or renal transplantation typically resolves uraemia-driven intractable hiccoughs.

High-dose intravenous or oral corticosteroids — commonly used in oncology, rheumatology, and transplantation — trigger hiccoughs in up to 5% of patients; dose reduction or medication substitution often resolves them.

Surgical manipulation near the phrenic nerve, diaphragm, or upper gastrointestinal tract, including oesophagectomy and cardiac surgery, predisposes to postoperative hiccoughs that are persistent rather than transient.

affectedDemographics: Adults of all ages for benign transient episodes; virtually universal
Men with persistent or intractable hiccoughs at roughly 4:1 ratio over women
Patients with GERD or oesophageal disease across all adult age groups
Patients on corticosteroids, opioids, or chemotherapy regimens
People with neurological disease including stroke, multiple sclerosis, and brain tumours
Patients with advanced chronic kidney disease and uraemia
Neonates and infants after feeding — physiologically normal and self-limiting
diagnosisOverview: For benign transient hiccoughs, no investigation is required. The evaluation of persistent hiccoughs (>48 hours) begins with a thorough history covering diet, alcohol intake, recent medications (especially corticosteroids, opioids, or chemotherapy), recent surgery, and associated neurological, thoracic, or gastrointestinal symptoms. Physical examination should assess for organomegaly, signs of peritoneal irritation, lymphadenopathy, and neurological abnormalities of the cranial nerves and long tracts. Initial investigations include basic blood chemistry to detect uraemia, hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, and hypercalcaemia. Chest radiography looks for mediastinal widening, pleural disease, pericardial effusion, or a pulmonary mass. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy or a barium swallow identifies oesophageal and gastric pathology including GERD, hiatal hernia, and oesophageal tumour. CT of the chest and abdomen is indicated in unexplained persistent cases to identify mediastinal, subphrenic, or retroperitoneal lesions pressing on the phrenic nerve. Brain MRI with attention to the posterior fossa and cervical cord is reserved for patients with any neurological signs, or when peripheral workup is unrevealing. The goal is to distinguish benign functional hiccoughs — treatable symptomatically — from hiccoughs that are a sentinel sign of a serious underlying condition.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects metabolic triggers including uraemia, hyponatraemia, hypokalaemia, and hypercalcaemia that lower the hiccough reflex threshold.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw from the arm; results return within a few hours and guide targeted treatment of any metabolic abnormality found.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for mediastinal masses, lung tumours, pleural or pericardial disease, and diaphragmatic abnormalities that could irritate the phrenic or vagus nerve.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A two-minute procedure standing at an X-ray plate; no preparation required. Results are reviewed by a radiologist within hours to a day.

diagnosticTests.test: Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy or barium swallow
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies GERD, oesophagitis, hiatal hernia, oesophageal tumour, or gastric pathology, the most common structural causes of persistent hiccoughs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Endoscopy requires fasting for at least six hours; a thin flexible camera passes through the mouth while you are lightly sedated. Results are available immediately. Barium swallow requires drinking a contrast liquid and takes 15-30 minutes with X-ray imaging.

diagnosticTests.test: CT thorax and abdomen with contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies mediastinal lymphadenopathy, aortic pathology, subphrenic abscess, hepatic or splenic enlargement, and other structural causes not visible on plain radiography.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 20-30 minute scan in a doughnut-shaped machine; an intravenous contrast dye injection causes a brief warm sensation. No eating for four hours beforehand is standard.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects brainstem infarcts, posterior fossa tumours, multiple sclerosis plaques, or cervical cord lesions responsible for intractable hiccoughs with a neurological mechanism.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute procedure in a tunnel-shaped magnet; loud knocking sounds are normal. No radiation. Contrast may be given intravenously.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Objectively documents acid reflux frequency and correlation with hiccough episodes when GERD is suspected but endoscopy is normal.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin catheter is passed through the nose and positioned in the oesophagus; it stays in place for 24 hours while you record meals, symptoms, and body position in a diary.

treatmentOverview: Management is structured around duration and cause. Benign transient hiccoughs require no treatment but can be shortened by several reflex-interrupting manoeuvres: breath-holding, breathing into a paper bag, swallowing granulated sugar, or applying cold water to the face, all of which act by altering phrenic or vagal tone. Persistent hiccoughs demand treatment of any identified underlying cause — acid suppression for GERD, dialysis intensification for uraemia, corticosteroid dose reduction, or surgical drainage for subphrenic abscess. When a cause is treated but hiccoughs persist, or while awaiting resolution, pharmacological therapy is used. Chlorpromazine, a dopamine antagonist and the only FDA-approved drug specifically for hiccoughs, is effective but carries sedation and extrapyramidal risks. Metoclopramide, a prokinetic with dopamine antagonism, is widely used first-line because of a more favourable tolerability profile. Baclofen, a GABA-B agonist that suppresses the hiccough reflex arc at spinal level, is increasingly preferred for persistent and intractable cases and is supported by randomised evidence. Gabapentin is used in neuropathic or centrally mediated hiccoughs. For truly intractable hiccoughs not responding to pharmacotherapy, phrenic nerve block or electrical stimulation of the nerve with an implanted device has produced sustained relief in case series. Non-pharmacological adjuncts including methylphenidate, acupuncture, and hypnosis have supportive anecdotal evidence. The key principle is to treat the cause first and to layer in pharmacological therapy when symptomatic control is needed.

medicalTreatments.name: Baclofen
medicalTreatments.description: A GABA-B receptor agonist that suppresses the hiccough reflex arc at the level of the spinal cord and brainstem. Used at 5-10 mg three times daily, titrated upward as tolerated. It is generally the preferred first-line drug for persistent and intractable hiccoughs in current practice because of moderate efficacy and a manageable side-effect profile compared with chlorpromazine.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials show hiccough resolution or marked reduction in approximately 70-80% of cases; response often seen within 48 hours of initiation.

medicalTreatments.name: Chlorpromazine (intramuscular or oral)
medicalTreatments.description: A first-generation antipsychotic with dopamine D2 blockade; the only agent with FDA approval specifically for intractable hiccoughs. Given as 25-50 mg intramuscularly for rapid effect or 25-50 mg orally three to four times daily for maintenance. Use limited by sedation, postural hypotension, and extrapyramidal reactions, particularly in older patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series and clinical experience suggest symptomatic relief in approximately 80% of intractable hiccoughs, though high-quality randomised data are limited.

medicalTreatments.name: Metoclopramide
medicalTreatments.description: A prokinetic dopamine antagonist that accelerates gastric emptying and reduces gastric distension, addressing one of the core peripheral triggers. Used at 10 mg three times daily before meals. A practical first choice when gastric distension or GERD is a suspected contributor.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Estimated response rate of 60-70% in gastric-distension-related persistent hiccoughs; less effective for central-mechanism intractable cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Gabapentin
medicalTreatments.description: An anticonvulsant that modulates calcium channels and reduces neuronal hyperexcitability in the hiccough reflex arc. Particularly useful when hiccoughs have a neuropathic or post-surgical aetiology. Doses of 300-900 mg three times daily are typical.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series report resolution in approximately 50-70% of neuropathic or post-chemotherapy hiccoughs where other agents have failed.

medicalTreatments.name: Proton pump inhibitors (e.g. omeprazole, lansoprazole)
medicalTreatments.description: Not directly anti-hiccough agents but essential when GERD or oesophagitis is the identified cause. Resolving the acid reflux removes the vagal irritation perpetuating the reflex arc.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In GERD-associated persistent hiccoughs, acid suppression resolves the episodes in the majority of cases; response typically seen within one to two weeks of effective acid control.

surgicalOptions.name: Phrenic nerve block
surgicalOptions.description: Injection of local anaesthetic around the phrenic nerve in the neck to temporarily interrupt afferent signals driving the hiccough reflex arc. Reserved for intractable cases that have failed pharmacological management.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Case reports describe temporary relief in the majority of treated patients, though effects last only hours to days; permanent phrenic nerve section is rarely performed due to the risk of diaphragmatic paralysis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Breath-holding and Valsalva manoeuvre
alternativeTreatments.description: Breath-holding raises carbon dioxide and alters phrenic nerve excitability; the Valsalva manoeuvre raises intrathoracic pressure. Both are first-line home remedies with wide anecdotal support and no risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Several small studies suggest that needle placement at specific points (particularly PC6 and ST36) can terminate acute hiccough bouts; evidence is insufficient for firm recommendations but the intervention is low-risk.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sipping ice water or swallowing granulated sugar
alternativeTreatments.description: Oropharyngeal stimulation by cold or sweet substances may interrupt the reflex arc via the vagus nerve; widely used as a first-line home measure for benign episodes.

whySeeSpecialist: Hiccoughs lasting beyond 48 hours warrant medical assessment to exclude a serious underlying cause. A gastroenterologist should be involved when GERD, oesophageal disease, or subphrenic pathology is suspected. A neurologist is needed when there are any neurological signs or when brain and cervical cord imaging identifies a lesion. A nephrologist should review cases where uraemia is identified. Intractable hiccoughs refractory to initial treatments are best managed by a physician experienced with phrenic nerve interventions or neuromodulation.
doctorSelectionGuide: For persistent or intractable hiccoughs, look for a general internist or gastroenterologist as the initial point of contact, since GERD and gastrointestinal disease account for the majority of identifiable causes. Ensure the clinician is willing to perform a systematic workup — blood chemistry, imaging, and endoscopy — before attributing symptoms to a functional cause. If neurological symptoms are present, direct referral to a neurologist with interest in movement disorders or autonomic neurology is appropriate. Ask whether the practice has access to 24-hour oesophageal pH monitoring and to an interventional pain physician experienced in phrenic nerve block.
preventionStrategies: Eat slowly and in smaller portions to reduce gastric distension, the most common trigger of benign hiccoughs.
Limit carbonated beverages and alcohol, both of which rapidly inflate the stomach.
Treat GERD promptly and consistently with acid suppression and lifestyle measures.
Discuss with your oncologist or rheumatologist whether hiccough-provoking medications such as high-dose corticosteroids can be tapered or substituted.
Maintain adequate electrolyte balance, particularly in the context of renal disease or aggressive diuretic therapy.
lifestyleModifications: Eat at a relaxed pace and avoid talking or laughing while swallowing.
Avoid overly hot or cold food and drinks consumed rapidly.
Maintain a healthy body weight to reduce GERD and diaphragmatic pressure from abdominal fat.
Avoid large meals immediately before lying down or sleeping.
Practise diaphragmatic breathing exercises, which may improve central regulation of the hiccough reflex.

recommended: Small, frequent meals rather than large infrequent ones to minimise gastric distension
Non-carbonated beverages such as still water or herbal teas
Soft, easily swallowed foods during active hiccough episodes to reduce aspiration risk
Adequate hydration, particularly important in patients with metabolic causes
avoid: Carbonated beverages — bubbles directly cause gastric distension and hiccough triggering
Alcohol, which causes oesophageal and gastric mucosal irritation and vagal stimulation
Extremely hot or cold foods consumed rapidly
Large meals, especially close to bedtime

exerciseGuidelines: Light physical activity is generally safe and can be continued during benign episodic hiccoughs. Vigorous exercise during an active prolonged bout is uncomfortable, and activities requiring sustained breath control — swimming, singing, wind instrument playing — should be temporarily avoided. Once the underlying cause of persistent hiccoughs has been identified and treated, unrestricted activity is appropriate.
dailyManagement: Keep a symptom diary noting hiccough frequency, duration, timing relative to meals, and any associated symptoms to assist clinical workup.
Take prescribed medications such as baclofen or metoclopramide consistently, not only when hiccoughs are bothersome.
Practise breath-holding or Valsalva manoeuvres at episode onset to interrupt brief bouts before they become prolonged.
Maintain regular meal timing and avoid skipping meals, which can lead to rapid eating when hunger builds.
Report any new neurological symptoms, worsening weight loss, or inability to maintain oral intake to your clinician promptly.
prognosis: Benign transient hiccoughs resolve spontaneously in virtually all cases within a few minutes to hours, with no lasting sequelae. Persistent hiccoughs (48 hours to one month) resolve in the majority of patients once the underlying cause is identified and treated, particularly when the trigger is GERD or a correctable metabolic disturbance. Intractable hiccoughs lasting more than one month carry a more variable prognosis that depends heavily on the aetiology: those driven by a reversible cause such as medication toxicity or uraemia often resolve completely with targeted treatment, while hiccoughs secondary to an inoperable central nervous system tumour or progressive neurological disease may remain refractory. In severe intractable cases, the condition causes significant morbidity through sleep disruption, weight loss, aspiration, and psychological distress, and it can persist for years. The longest documented case of intractable hiccoughs in the medical literature ran for over 60 years. With modern pharmacological and interventional options, most patients achieve acceptable symptom control even when complete resolution is not possible.
recoveryTimeline: Benign hiccoughs stop within minutes to a few hours. Persistent hiccoughs treated for an identifiable cause — GERD, metabolic disturbance, or medication withdrawal — typically resolve within one to two weeks of effective treatment. Intractable hiccoughs may require several medication trials over weeks before an effective regimen is found. Phrenic nerve block provides relief measurable in hours but lasting only days; more durable pharmacological regimens may take two to four weeks to titrate to full effect.
complications: Sleep disruption — persistent hiccoughs throughout the night cause insomnia and cumulative fatigue, impairing cognitive function and work performance.
Malnutrition and weight loss — difficulty eating and swallowing during active episodes, combined with nausea, can reduce oral intake substantially in intractable cases.
Aspiration — recurrent hiccoughs during or after meals can propel gastric contents toward the airway, raising the risk of aspiration pneumonia, particularly in patients with neurological disease.
Psychological distress — embarrassment, anxiety, social withdrawal, and depression are commonly reported by patients with intractable hiccoughs and should be assessed and addressed.
Oesophageal trauma — very forceful or prolonged hiccoughing has been associated with oesophageal rupture (Boerhaave-type injury) in rare case reports.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Digestive Diseases Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resource from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases covering gastrointestinal conditions including GERD, a common cause of persistent hiccoughs.

supportResources.name: NHS — Hiccups
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on hiccoughs, covering self-care measures, when to seek medical help, and available treatments.

sources.title: Steger M et al. Systemic review of the pathophysiology and pharmacological treatment of hiccups. JAMA Internal Medicine, 2015
Becker DE. Nausea, vomiting and hiccups: a review of mechanisms and treatment. Anesthesia Progress, 2010
Petroianu G et al. Treatment of hiccup: review of evidence. European Journal of Internal Medicine, 2019
Chang FY, Lu CL. Hiccup: mystery, nature and treatment. Journal of Neurogastroenterology and Motility, 2012
NHS — Hiccups: causes and treatment overview

Moretto EN et al. Interventions for treating persistent and intractable hiccups in adults (Cochrane Review overview). Cochrane Database, 2013

confusedWithConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GERD is a common cause of persistent hiccoughs rather than a mimic; heartburn, regurgitation, and a positive response to acid suppression help distinguish GERD-driven hiccoughs from those with a central or structural cause.

confusedWithConditions.name: Epilepsy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Myoclonic epileptic jerks can superficially resemble hiccoughs but involve limb and trunk musculature in addition to the diaphragm, are associated with EEG changes, and do not produce the glottic sound characteristic of a true hiccough.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthmatic breathing irregularity and cough can be confused with hiccoughs in children; asthma produces wheeze on auscultation, responds to bronchodilators, and lacks the rhythmic diaphragmatic spasm of hiccoughs.

relatedConditions.name: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (without oesophagitis)
relatedConditions.relevance: GERD is one of the most common identifiable causes of persistent hiccoughs, through vagal irritation from oesophageal acid exposure.

relatedConditions.name: Disorders of the vagus nerve
relatedConditions.relevance: The vagus nerve forms a key afferent limb of the hiccough reflex arc; structural or functional vagal pathology is an important cause of persistent hiccoughs.

relatedConditions.name: Disorders of the diaphragm
relatedConditions.relevance: Any intrinsic diaphragmatic disease, including diaphragmatic hernia or diaphragmitis, can directly irritate the phrenic nerve and produce persistent hiccoughs.

relatedConditions.name: Metabolic encephalopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: Metabolic brain dysfunction — including uraemia and electrolyte disturbance — lowers the threshold for the hiccough reflex and is an important cause of intractable episodes.

relatedConditions.name: Brain tumour (glioma)
relatedConditions.relevance: Posterior fossa and brainstem tumours can directly compress the hiccough centre, producing intractable hiccoughs as a presenting or evolving symptom.

faqs.question: What causes hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: Most hiccoughs are triggered by gastric distension from eating or drinking rapidly, swallowing air, or consuming carbonated beverages. These stimulate the phrenic or vagus nerve, causing involuntary diaphragmatic spasms. Persistent hiccoughs (over 48 hours) need investigation for GERD, metabolic disturbances, medications, or neurological causes.

faqs.question: When are hiccoughs a sign of something serious?
faqs.answer: Hiccoughs lasting more than 48 hours are considered persistent and warrant medical assessment. Intractable hiccoughs beyond one month can signal brain or spinal cord lesions, severe metabolic disease, subphrenic abscess, or thoracic tumours pressing on the phrenic nerve.

faqs.question: How do I stop hiccoughs quickly at home?
faqs.answer: Breath-holding, breathing into a paper bag, swallowing granulated sugar, sipping cold water, or performing the Valsalva manoeuvre (bearing down as if having a bowel movement) can interrupt the reflex arc and stop brief hiccough bouts within minutes.

faqs.question: What medication is used for persistent hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: Baclofen (a muscle relaxant) and metoclopramide (a prokinetic) are commonly used first-line for persistent hiccoughs. Chlorpromazine is the only FDA-approved drug specifically for intractable hiccoughs. Gabapentin is preferred when a neuropathic mechanism is suspected.

faqs.question: Can GERD cause hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acid reflux irritates the lower oesophageal lining and stimulates vagal nerve fibres, activating the hiccough reflex arc. GERD is one of the most common identifiable causes of persistent hiccoughs, and treating the reflux with acid-suppressing medication commonly resolves them.

faqs.question: How long do hiccoughs normally last?
faqs.answer: Benign hiccoughs typically last between a few minutes and 48 hours and resolve without treatment. Those lasting 48 hours to one month are classified as persistent, and episodes beyond one month are called intractable. Most people experience only the brief benign form.

faqs.question: Can hiccoughs be a symptom of a brain problem?
faqs.answer: Yes. Brainstem strokes, posterior fossa tumours, and multiple sclerosis plaques can produce persistent or intractable hiccoughs by disrupting the neural circuitry that normally suppresses the hiccough reflex. Any hiccoughs lasting more than 48 hours with neurological symptoms need urgent brain imaging.

faqs.question: Are hiccoughs dangerous?
faqs.answer: Brief hiccoughs are harmless. Prolonged intractable hiccoughs can cause sleep disruption, weight loss from difficulty eating, aspiration risk, and significant psychological distress. Very rarely, violent prolonged hiccoughing has been associated with oesophageal injury.

faqs.question: Why do I get hiccoughs after drinking alcohol?
faqs.answer: Alcohol causes rapid gastric distension and directly irritates the oesophageal and gastric lining, stimulating the vagal and phrenic nerve fibres that trigger the hiccough reflex. Chronic heavy alcohol use can additionally cause oesophagitis and central nervous system effects that sustain hiccoughs.

faqs.question: Can medications cause hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Corticosteroids (especially intravenous high-dose regimens), opioid pain medications, benzodiazepines, and several chemotherapy agents including cisplatin are well-documented hiccough triggers. If hiccoughs start soon after a medication change, review with your prescriber.

faqs.question: Do babies hiccough a lot?
faqs.answer: Hiccoughs are very common and normal in newborns and infants, particularly after feeding. They occur because the central nervous system and diaphragmatic reflex control are still maturing. Neonatal hiccoughs are not harmful and resolve without intervention as the baby develops.

faqs.question: What blood tests are done for persistent hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: A basic metabolic panel is ordered first: electrolytes (sodium, potassium), calcium, blood urea nitrogen, and creatinine. Elevated urea (uraemia) and electrolyte abnormalities such as low sodium or potassium lower the hiccough reflex threshold and are treatable causes.

faqs.question: Can hiccoughs cause weight loss?
faqs.answer: In intractable cases, yes. Continuous hiccoughs make it difficult and uncomfortable to eat, chew, and swallow, reducing food intake. Combined with nausea and sleep disruption, prolonged intractable hiccoughs can cause clinically significant weight loss requiring nutritional support.

faqs.question: What is a phrenic nerve block for hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: A phrenic nerve block involves injecting local anaesthetic around the phrenic nerve in the neck under ultrasound guidance. This temporarily interrupts the nerve signals driving the diaphragmatic spasms. It is reserved for intractable cases that have failed multiple medications, providing hours to days of relief.

faqs.question: Can stress or anxiety cause hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: Emotional excitement and stress can trigger brief hiccough episodes in healthy people, likely through central effects on the hiccough reflex centre and changes in breathing pattern and gastric tone. However, psychological causes rarely explain truly persistent hiccoughs, which require medical evaluation.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if hiccoughs last more than 48 hours, recur frequently, disrupt sleep or eating, or accompany other symptoms such as chest pain, difficulty swallowing, weight loss, headache, or any neurological change. These are signs the hiccoughs may have an underlying treatable cause.

faqs.question: Is baclofen safe for hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: Baclofen is generally well tolerated at the doses used for hiccoughs (5-10 mg three times daily). Common side effects include drowsiness and dizziness, particularly at the start of treatment or with dose increases. It should be used cautiously in elderly patients and those with renal impairment, and it must not be stopped abruptly after prolonged use.

faqs.question: Can hiccoughs come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hiccoughs can recur if the underlying cause is not fully treated or if a precipitating factor such as GERD or corticosteroid use persists. Patients with neurological or metabolic causes should have the root condition managed long-term to minimise recurrence.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hiccough and hiccup?
faqs.answer: Hiccough and hiccup refer to the same physiological event — the spelling 'hiccough' is the traditional medical and British English form, while 'hiccup' is the more common lay and American spelling. The ICD-10 code R06.6 uses the spelling 'hiccough'. Both terms are fully interchangeable.

faqs.question: Can kidney disease cause hiccoughs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Advanced chronic kidney disease causes uraemia — the buildup of metabolic waste products — which lowers the threshold for involuntary reflexes including the hiccough arc. Uraemic hiccoughs are a recognised complication of renal failure and often improve with dialysis or renal replacement therapy.

metaDescription: Hiccoughs lasting over 48 hours can signal GERD, nerve irritation or metabolic disease. Learn causes, treatments and when to see a doctor. Reviewed.
